title: Final Paper Outline Example
text:
Your final paper should be approximately 10–15 pages (double-spaced, 12pt font, 1-inch margins), not counting tables, figures, or references. It should read as a coherent research report — not a problem set — with clear prose connecting each section.
The outline below follows the example research question from the proposal assignment: Does parental education influence children’s educational attainment, and does this relationship differ by gender? Your paper should follow this same structure, adapted to your own question, data, and findings.

title: Final Paper Outline Example
text:
Your final paper should be approximately 10–15 pages (double-spaced, 12pt font, 1-inch margins), not counting tables, figures, or references. It should read as a coherent research report — not a problem set — with clear prose connecting each section.
The outline below follows the example research question from the proposal assignment: Does parental education influence children’s educational attainment, and does this relationship differ by gender? Your paper should follow this same structure, adapted to your own question, data, and findings.

title: Final Paper Outline Example
text:
1. Introduction (1–2 pages)
Open by motivating your topic for a general reader. By the end of this section, the reader should know:

What you are studying and why it matters sociologically
The gap or puzzle your paper addresses (what do we not yet know, or what is contested?)
Your research question stated clearly and directly
A brief preview of your data, method, and main findings


Example: This paper examines whether parental education shapes children’s educational attainment, and whether this relationship differs by the gender of parent and child. Using data from the General Social Survey, I find that…

title: Final Paper Outline Example
text:
2. Background and Theory (2–3 pages)
Situate your question in the existing literature. You should draw on your annotated bibliography here. This section should:

Summarize what prior research has found on your topic
Identify the theoretical framework(s) you are using to make sense of your question
Derive your hypotheses from theory — explain why you expect what you expect


Example: Status attainment research consistently shows that parental education is one of the strongest predictors of children’s schooling (Blau and Duncan 1967; Sewell et al. 1969). Two competing theoretical models make different predictions about gender-differentiated effects…

End this section with your hypotheses stated explicitly, as you did in the proposal.

title: Final Paper Outline Example
text:
3. Data and Methods (2–3 pages)
Describe exactly how you will answer your research question. A reader should be able to replicate your analysis from this section alone. Cover:

Data source: What dataset are you using? Who collected it, when, and how? What is the unit of analysis?
Sample: Any restrictions you impose (e.g., age range, year, non-missing on key variables) and your final analytic sample size
Variables: For each variable, state its name, what it measures, and how it is coded. Organize by role: dependent variable, key independent variable(s), moderating variable(s) if any, control variables
Analytical strategy: What statistical method(s) are you using (OLS regression, logistic regression, etc.)? Why is this method appropriate for your outcome variable and research question?


Example: I use the “attain” subset of the GSS, which includes N = X respondents with complete data on all key variables. My dependent variable is respondent’s years of completed education (educ). My key independent variables are father’s education (paeduc) and mother’s education (maeduc), both measured in years…

title: Final Paper Outline Example
text:
4. Results (3–4 pages)
Present your findings in order from simple to complex. Include at least one table and one figure.

4.1 Descriptive Statistics
A table showing means (or proportions) and standard deviations for all key variables. Briefly narrate what the numbers show about your sample.


4.2 Main Analysis
Present your regression results in a formatted table (use modelsummary() in R). Walk the reader through the key coefficients: sign, magnitude, and statistical significance. Relate each finding back to your hypotheses — do the results support or contradict them?


4.3 Additional Analyses (if applicable)
If you examine interactions, subgroups, or run robustness checks, present them here. A visualization (e.g., a predicted probability plot or a coefficient plot) is encouraged.

Example: Table 2 presents OLS regression coefficients for three models. In Model 1, each additional year of father’s education is associated with 0.32 more years of the respondent’s education (p < .001). Adding mother’s education in Model 2 reduces this coefficient slightly…

title: Final Paper Outline Example
text:
5. Discussion (2–3 pages)
Interpret your results — this is where you do the sociological work of making sense of what you found. Address:

Do your findings support your hypotheses? For each hypothesis, explain why or why not.
What do the results mean substantively? Don’t just report the numbers again; explain what they tell us about the social world.
Limitations: What are the weaknesses of your data or method? What alternative explanations can you not rule out?
Future directions: What would a follow-up study need to do to address these limitations?

title: Final Paper Outline Example
text:
6. Conclusion (0.5–1 page)
Briefly restate your research question, main findings, and takeaway. What should the reader remember? Connect back to the broader sociological significance you outlined in the introduction.

title: Final Paper Outline Example
text:
References
List all sources you cited, formatted consistently (ASA or APA style is fine — just be consistent). Every source in the text should appear here, and every entry here should be cited in the text.

title: Final Paper Outline Example
section: A Note on Tables and Figures
text:
A Note on Tables and Figures

All tables and figures should be numbered and have a descriptive title
Every table and figure should be referenced in the text (e.g., “As shown in Table 1…”)
Tables should be formatted cleanly — use modelsummary() or knitr::kable(), not raw R output
Figures should have labeled axes and a clear legend if needed

title: Week 14
text:
Housekeeping:

Final paper, HW #11, In-class presentations

Statistical content — three parts:

Part 1: Mediation vs. moderation — getting the concepts straight
Part 2: Mediation analysis — path models, Baron & Kenny, R implementation
Part 3: Multicollinearity — when predictors are too similar

In-class lab:

Mediation analysis on your own research data

title: Section I: Multiple Choice
text:
SOC 106 Final Exam Key


May 11, 2026



Name: ________________________________



Directions: This is a closed-book, closed-computer final exam. You have a total of 3 hours to complete the exam, but it may not necessarily take the entire exam period. For the multiple-choice section, circle the best answer; for each short-answer question, be sure to answer all parts (a-c, where applicable) and provide all information requested. All output you need has been provided, and there is a reference sheet on the last page with any additional information/formulas you might need. Each multiple-choice question is worth 0.5 points and each short-answer question is worth 1.5 points, for a total of 20 points on the exam.

Section I: Multiple Choice
For each question, circle the best answer. There are 10 questions total, each worth 0.5 points, for a total of 5 points.
Question 1. A researcher records each respondent’s highest educational attainment using four categories: HS or less, Some college, College graduate, and Graduate degree. What type of variable is this?

A. Continuous
B. Binary categorical
C. Ordinal categorical
D. Count

Correct answer: C. These categories have a natural order from lower to higher educational attainment, so this is an ordinal categorical variable.
Question 2. Suppose you are asked to model whether a respondent is a homeowner (1 = yes, 0 = no) using a model designed for a binary outcome. Which probability distribution is the most appropriate?

A. Normal
B. Binomial
C. Poisson
D. Uniform

Correct answer: B. A yes-or-no outcome is binary, so the binomial distribution is the most appropriate choice.
Question 3. Look at the four miniature distributions below. Which panel shows a left-skewed distribution?










A. Panel A
B. Panel B
C. Panel C
D. Panel D

Correct answer: B. Panel B has most observations concentrated at higher values with a longer tail stretching to the left.
Question 4. Which statement best describes the Central Limit Theorem?

A. With a large enough random sample, the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately normal even if the population itself is not normal
B. Any single large sample will always look perfectly normal
C. The population must be normal before you can estimate a mean
D. Increasing the sample size makes all estimates unbiased

Correct answer: A. The CLT is about the sampling distribution of sample means, not the shape of every raw sample.
Question 5. Under a normal curve, about what proportion of observations fall within two standard deviations of the mean?

A. About 68 percent
B. About 80 percent
C. About 95 percent
D. About 99.7 percent

Correct answer: C. The 68-95-99.7 rule says about 95 percent of observations fall within two standard deviations of the mean.
Question 6. You want to show the distribution of a categorical variable such as marital status. Which figure is usually the best choice?

A. Histogram
B. Scatterplot
C. Bar chart
D. Boxplot

Correct answer: C. Bar charts are the standard choice for showing counts or proportions for categorical variables.
Question 7. A researcher uses 1,500 GSS respondents to estimate the national homeownership rate. Which pair of terms correctly describes the 1,500 respondents and all U.S. adults, respectively?

A. Population; sample
B. Sample; population
C. Parameter; statistic
D. Statistic; parameter

Correct answer: B. The observed respondents are the sample, and the broader group the researcher wants to generalize to is the population.
Question 8. A researcher wants to test whether Black adults and white adults report different mean occupational prestige scores. Which hypothesis test is the most appropriate?

A. Chi-square test of independence B. One-sample t-test C. Two-sample t-test D. Correlation test

Correct answer: C. A two-sample t-test compares the means of two groups on a quantitative outcome.
Question 9. A p-value of 0.03 means:

A. There is a 3 percent chance the null hypothesis is false
B. If the null hypothesis were true, there would be a 3 percent chance of observing results at least this extreme
C. The alternative hypothesis is true with 97 percent probability
D. Exactly 3 percent of the sample supports the null hypothesis

Correct answer: B. This matches the course phrasing that a p-value is the probability of observing results at least this extreme, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
Question 10. What does an R^2 statistic tell you in an OLS regression?

A. Whether each coefficient is statistically significant
B. The exact causal effect of the key independent variable
C. The proportion of the variation in the outcome explained by the model
D. The probability that the null hypothesis is true

Correct answer: C. R-squared tells you how much of the variation in the outcome is explained by the model.



Section II: Short Answer
For each question, be sure to answer all parts (a-c) and provide all information requested. There are a total of 10 questions, with each question worth 1.5 points, for a total of 15 points.
Question 11. Two-Sample t-Test (1.5 points)
A researcher wants to know whether men and women reported different average hours worked last week in the 2024 GSS. The following output summarizes a two-sample t-test comparing mean hours worked across the two groups.
Two Sample t-test

data: hrs1 by sex
t = -6.46, df = 2186, p-value < 0.001
alternative hypothesis: true difference in mean is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -6.95 -3.71
sample estimates:
mean in group Men   42.5
mean in group Women 37.2

a) Which group worked more hours on average, and by about how many hours?
Template answer: Men worked more hours on average. The mean was 42.5 hours for men and 37.2 hours for women, so the gap is about 5.3 hours.
Rubric: 0.5 points for identifying men as the higher-mean group and describing the gap as about 5.3 hours.
b) Is the difference statistically significant at the 0.05 level? Briefly explain how you know this.
Template answer: Yes. The p-value is < 0.001, which is below 0.05, and the 95 percent confidence interval for the difference does not include 0, so we would reject the null hypothesis of no mean difference.
Rubric: 0.5 points for stating that the result is statistically significant and linking that conclusion to the p-value or confidence interval.
c) Write 1-2 sentences interpreting what these results mean in substantive terms.
Template answer: In this sample, men reported working more hours last week than women. The gap is statistically significant and substantively noticeable, at a little over five hours on average.
Rubric: 0.5 points for a clear substantive interpretation that identifies both the direction and size of the difference.

Question 12. OLS with a Categorical Predictor (1.5 points)
A researcher wants to know whether occupational prestige differs across educational attainment, controlling for age and sex. Table 2 reports an OLS model predicting occupational prestige (prestg10) from educational attainment, age, and sex. Education has been grouped into four categories: HS or less, Some college, College graduate, and Graduate degree.

Table 2. OLS model predicting occupational prestige





Term


Prestige score






Intercept


46.76***






(0.71)




Age


0.09***






(0.01)




Female (1 = yes)


-0.92*






(0.39)




HS or less


-13.70***






(0.54)




Some college


-8.58***






(0.56)




Graduate degree


2.77***






(0.63)




Num. Obs.


3611




R2


0.259




Adj. R2


0.258




AIC


29492.7






Note.   * p < .05, ** p < .01, *** p < .001.





a) Which education category is the baseline/reference group in this model? How can you tell?
Template answer: College graduate is the baseline/reference group because it is omitted from the coefficient list and absorbed into the intercept.
Rubric: 0.5 points for correctly identifying college graduate as the baseline/reference category.
b) Interpret the coefficient for HS or less.
Template answer: Holding age and sex constant, respondents with HS or less are predicted to score about 13.70 points lower on occupational prestige than college graduates.
Rubric: 0.5 points for interpreting the HS or less coefficient relative to the college graduate baseline.
c) What does the R^2 tell you about this model?
Template answer: The R2 is about 0.26, which means the model explains roughly 25.9 percent of the variation in occupational prestige.
Rubric: 0.5 points for interpreting R2 as the share of variation in the outcome explained by the model.

Question 13. Logistic Regression in Odds Ratios (1.5 points)
A researcher wants to know whether homeownership is related to education, age, and sex. Table 3 reports a logistic regression predicting homeownership. Coefficients are shown as odds ratios.

Table 3. Logistic regression predicting homeownership





Term


Odds ratio






Intercept


0.08***






(0.02)




Education (years)


1.13***






(0.02)




Age


1.04***






(0.00)




Female (1 = yes)


0.72***






(0.07)




Num. Obs.


2489




AIC


2738.1






Note.   * p < .05, ** p < .01, *** p < .001.





a) Interpret the odds ratio for Education (years).
Template answer: Holding age and sex constant, each additional year of education is associated with odds of homeownership that are about 13.2 percent higher.
Rubric: 0.5 points for interpreting the education odds ratio as an increase in the odds of homeownership.
b) Interpret the odds ratio for Age.
Template answer: Holding education and sex constant, each additional year of age is associated with odds of homeownership that are about 3.7 percent higher.
Rubric: 0.5 points for interpreting the age odds ratio as a positive increase in the odds of homeownership.
c) Interpret the odds ratio for Female (1 = yes).
Template answer: Holding education and age constant, women have odds of homeownership that are about 28.0 percent lower than men.
Rubric: 0.5 points for identifying men as the reference group and correctly interpreting the direction of the odds ratio.

Question 14. Interaction with Two Continuous Variables (1.5 points)
A researcher wants to know whether the relationship between education and hours worked last week depends on age. Table 4 reports an OLS model predicting hours worked from education, age, sex, and an education-age interaction.

Table 4. OLS model with an education x age interaction





Term


Hours worked






Intercept


25.87***






(5.08)




Education (years)


1.19***






(0.35)




Age


0.40***






(0.11)




Education x Age


-0.03***






(0.01)




Female (1 = yes)


-5.18***






(0.59)




Num. Obs.


2120




R2


0.041




Adj. R2


0.039




AIC


18045.7






Note.   * p < .05, ** p < .01, *** p < .001.





a) Is the interaction statistically significant and how do you know?
Template answer: Yes. The interaction is statistically significant because the education-age term has asterisks next to the coefficient in the table, indicating p < .05 or smaller.
Rubric: 0.5 points for stating that the interaction is statistically significant and explaining that the stars or p-value show this.
b) Based on the sign of the interaction term, does the association between education and hours worked become stronger or weaker as age increases?
Template answer: It becomes weaker as age increases. The negative interaction means the slope of education gets smaller, and eventually more negative, at older ages.
Rubric: 0.5 points for recognizing that the negative interaction implies a weaker education-hours relationship at older ages.
c) Write one substantive sentence interpreting the interaction.
Template answer: The association between education and hours worked depends on age: education is linked to slightly more hours among younger adults, but that relationship weakens and can turn negative at older ages.
Rubric: 0.5 points for a clear substantive interpretation of the continuous-by-continuous interaction.

Question 15. Interaction with One Categorical Variable (1.5 points)
A researcher wants to know whether the association between education and occupational prestige differs between married respondents and respondents who are not married. Table 5 reports an OLS model predicting occupational prestige from education, marital status, age, sex, and an education-married interaction.

Table 5. OLS model with an education x married interaction





Term


Prestige score






Intercept


10.95***






(1.48)




Age


0.09***






(0.01)




Female (1 = yes)


-0.62






(0.39)




Education (years)


1.99***






(0.10)




Married (1 = yes)


-2.01






(1.99)




Education x Married


0.28*






(0.14)




Num. Obs.


3604




R2


0.245




Adj. R2


0.244




AIC


29501.2






Note.   * p < .05, ** p < .01, *** p < .001.





a) What does the coefficient on Education (years) tell you?
Template answer: Among respondents who are not married, each additional year of education is associated with about 1.99 more points of occupational prestige, holding age and sex constant.
Rubric: 0.5 points for interpreting the education lower-order term for the reference group.
b) What does the coefficient on Married (1 = yes) tell you in this uncentered model?
Template answer: Because this model is not centered, the married coefficient is the predicted married-versus-not-married difference when education equals 0. At that value, married respondents are predicted to score about 2.01 points lower on occupational prestige than respondents who are not married, holding age and sex constant.
Rubric: 0.5 points for interpreting the married lower-order term in the uncentered model.
c) Interpret the interaction, including both the lower-order terms and the higher-order term.
Template answer: For respondents who are not married, the education slope is about 1.99 prestige points per additional year of education. For married respondents, the slope is steeper by about 0.28 points, so their education slope is about 2.27. In other words, the prestige gap associated with education grows faster among married respondents.
Rubric: 0.5 points for using the lower-order and higher-order terms together to explain how the education slope differs by marital status.

Question 16. Margins Plot (1.5 points)
Figure 1 shows predicted hours worked from the model shown in Table 4, in which the relationship between education and hours worked is allowed to vary by age. The lines are shown for ages 30, 45, and 60, holding sex constant.










a) Describe the pattern for respondents age 30 as education increases.
Template answer: For respondents age 30, predicted hours worked increase as education rises, from about 40.4 hours at 8 years of education to about 43.2 hours at 16 years.
Rubric: 0.5 points for identifying the upward pattern for the age-30 line.
b) Describe the pattern for respondents age 60 as education increases.
Template answer: For respondents age 60, predicted hours worked decrease as education rises, from about 45.5 hours at 8 years of education to about 41.4 hours at 16 years.
Rubric: 0.5 points for identifying the downward pattern for the age-60 line.
c) What does the pattern of the lines suggest about moderation?
Template answer: The lines are not parallel, so the plot suggests moderation: the relationship between education and hours worked changes across age groups.
Rubric: 0.5 points for connecting non-parallel lines to moderation.

Question 17. Sampling Design (1.5 points)
A research team wants to estimate the share of U.S. adults who are currently employed full-time. Rather than drawing names from a complete list of all U.S. adults, they divide the country into geographic regions, randomly select 50 regions, and then attempt to interview every adult they can reach within each selected region. They are only able to reach adults who are home during weekday business hours.

a) What type of probability sampling design does this describe? Briefly explain how you identified it.
Template answer: This is cluster sampling. The sampling unit is a geographic area (cluster) rather than an individual; all reachable adults within each selected cluster are interviewed.
Rubric: 0.5 points for correctly naming cluster sampling and briefly explaining why.
b) What type of bias does restricting interviews to weekday business hours introduce, and why is it a problem for this particular study?
Template answer: This introduces non-response bias (or selection bias). Adults who are home on weekday mornings or afternoons are systematically different from those who are not — people employed full-time are far less likely to be reachable, so the sample will underrepresent full-time workers and likely underestimate the full-time employment rate.
Rubric: 0.5 points for identifying non-response or selection bias and explaining that employed adults are systematically less reachable.
c) In 1–2 sentences, explain what it would mean for the final sample to be representative of the U.S. adult population.
Template answer: A representative sample is one whose characteristics — such as age, sex, race, region, and employment status — closely mirror those of the broader U.S. adult population, so that estimates from the sample can be generalized to all U.S. adults.
Rubric: 0.5 points for defining representativeness in terms of the sample’s characteristics matching the population.

Question 18. Mediation or Moderation? (1.5 points)
A researcher first finds that education is positively associated with homeownership. After adding income to the model, the coefficient for education becomes much smaller. The researcher argues that part of the association between education and homeownership operates through income.

a) Is this pattern more consistent with mediation or moderation?
Template answer: This pattern is more consistent with mediation.
Rubric: 0.5 points for correctly identifying the pattern as mediation.
b) Explain why in 1-2 sentences.
Template answer: It is mediation because income is being treated as the mechanism that helps carry the effect of education to homeownership. The key clue is that the education coefficient shrinks after income is added, which is consistent with a pathway story rather than a different-effect-for-different-groups story.
Rubric: 0.5 points for explaining that income is the mechanism or pathway linking education to homeownership.
c) Draw a simple path diagram with arrows to show the claim.
Template answer: Education -> Income -> Homeownership
Rubric: 0.5 points for a correct path diagram showing income between education and homeownership.

Question 19. Figure Critique (1.5 points)
Figure 2 contains several presentation problems.










a) Identify one problem with the figure and briefly explain why it is a problem.
Template answer: One problem is that it uses a line graph for a categorical x-axis (men versus women), when a bar chart or clearly labeled points would be more appropriate.
Rubric: 0.5 points for identifying one genuine design problem and explaining it.
b) Identify a second problem with the figure and briefly explain why it is a problem.
Template answer: A second problem is that the y-axis starts at 60 instead of 0, which exaggerates the apparent difference between the two groups.
Rubric: 0.5 points for identifying a second genuine design problem and explaining it.
c) Identify a third problem with the figure and briefly explain why it is a problem.
Template answer: A third problem is that the title is misleading because it claims women are much more likely to own homes even though the plotted values show the opposite pattern. Another acceptable answer is that the y-axis label says ‘Number of homeowners’ even though the graph shows percentages.
Rubric: 0.5 points for identifying a third genuine design problem and explaining it.

Question 20. Confidence Interval Interpretation (1.5 points)
A researcher wants to estimate the share of adults in the population who are currently married. Table 7 reports the point estimate and confidence interval.

Table 7. Estimated share of adults who are currently married





Point estimate


95% confidence interval






51.1%


[48.8%, 53.3%]





a) What does the point estimate mean in words?
Template answer: The point estimate means that about 51.1% of adults are estimated to be currently married.
Rubric: 0.5 points for correctly stating what the point estimate represents.
b) Interpret the 95 percent confidence interval in plain language.
Template answer: We are 95 percent confident that the true population share of adults who are currently married is between 48.8% and 53.3%.
Rubric: 0.5 points for a correct plain-language interpretation of the interval.
c) If the sample size were larger, holding everything else constant, would you expect the interval to become wider or narrower? Why?
Template answer: It would usually become narrower because a larger sample size reduces sampling variability, which shrinks the standard error and the margin of error.
Rubric: 0.5 points for stating that the interval would narrow and linking that to a smaller standard error or margin of error.

title: Week 16
text:
Agenda

Final exam format and logistics
The major concept areas you should be ready for
How to study efficiently during the final week without trying to memorize everything




Big picture


This review is intentionally high-level. The goal is to help you organize your studying and recognize the kinds of reasoning the exam will ask for, not to preview exact questions.

title: Week 16
text:
Final Exam Format

Date: Monday, May 11, 2026
Time: 11:30 AM to 2:30 PM
Place: Dwinelle 246 (Here)
Format: closed-book, closed-computer
Structure: 10 multiple-choice questions and 10 short-answer questions
Points: 20 points total
What you’ll get: all output you need is provided for you




What this means for studying


Focus on concepts, interpretation, and choosing the right tool for the job. You do not need to memorize long stretches of code.

title: Week 16
section: What You Should Be Ready To Do
text:
What You Should Be Ready To Do


Multiple choice

Identify variable types
Choose an appropriate probability distribution
Recognize skew and the normal-curve rules
Pick the right figure or test
Distinguish population from sample
Interpret a p-value or R^2


Short answer

Read a t-test/chi-square test
Interpret regression output
Read and interpret interaction terms and margins plots
Explain what moderation means and interpret an interaction coefficient
Evaluate sampling designs and representativeness
Critique a misleading figure
Interpret a confidence interval

title: Week 16
text:
Core Concepts to Review








Topic
What to remember
Quick example




Variable type
Ask: is it continuous, binary, nominal, or ordinal?
Education levels with a clear order are ordinal


Distribution
Match the distribution to the outcome
A yes/no outcome fits a binomial setup


Shape
Left-skew means the tail stretches to the left
Most observations are high, with a long lower tail


Figure choice
Match the figure to the variable structure
One categorical variable -> bar chart


Sample vs. population
The sample is observed; the population is what you want to generalize to
1,500 respondents vs. all U.S. adults






A good self-check


If I changed the variable type or the outcome, would my graph, model, or test also need to change?

title: Week 16
text:
Choosing the Right Tool








Goal
What the variables look like
Good choice




Show the distribution of one categorical variable
One categorical variable
Bar chart


Show the distribution of one quantitative variable
One continuous/count variable
Histogram


Compare the mean of a quantitative outcome across two groups
Quantitative Y + binary group
Two-sample t-test


Study the relationship between two categorical variables
Categorical X + categorical Y
Chi-square test


Model a continuous outcome
Quantitative Y
OLS regression


Model a binary outcome
Y = 0/1
Logistic regression






On the exam


Many mistakes come from choosing the wrong tool because the student did not first identify the outcome type.

title: Week 16
text:
Inference Refresher

The 68-95-99.7 rule only applies to an approximately normal distribution
The Central Limit Theorem is about the sampling distribution of sample means, not about every raw sample looking normal
A margin of error is the plus-or-minus amount around an estimate
A confidence interval is the full range: estimate plus or minus the margin of error
Holding everything else constant, a larger sample size usually makes a confidence interval narrower
A higher confidence level usually makes a confidence interval wider




P-value wording to know well


A p-value is the probability of getting results this extreme, or more extreme, if the null hypothesis were true.
For any output you read on the exam: identify the direction, comment on statistical significance, then interpret substantively in plain language.

title: Week 16
section: Reading a t-Test or Confidence Interval
text:
Reading a t-Test or Confidence Interval
Group A mean = 38.2
Group B mean = 34.7
p-value = 0.02
95% CI for the difference = [1.0, 6.0]

First, identify which group is higher and by about how much
Then ask whether the result is statistically significant
Use the p-value or the confidence interval to justify your answer
Finally, write 1-2 sentences saying what the result means in plain language




A strong short answer usually does three things


It identifies the direction, comments on statistical significance, and then interprets the finding substantively.

title: Week 16
text:
Reading OLS Output



Term
Estimate




Intercept
5.8***


Divorced
-0.9**


Widowed
-0.6


Never married
-1.2***


Age
0.02*


R^2
0.16



Outcome: life satisfaction (1–7 scale). Married is the omitted baseline.

In OLS, the outcome is continuous
If one category is missing from the table, that missing category is the baseline/reference group
A coefficient tells you the expected difference in the outcome, holding the other variables constant
R^2 tells you how much of the variation in the outcome is explained by the model




Study move


Practice saying coefficient interpretations out loud using the phrase “holding the other variables constant.”






Sample interpretation — Never married


Respondents who have never been married are predicted to score 1.2 points lower on life satisfaction than married respondents (the reference group), holding age constant.

title: Week 16
text:
Reading Logistic Regression Output



Term
Odds ratio




Income (thousands)
1.02**


Age
1.03***


Married (1 = yes)
1.38*



Outcome: whether the respondent participates in a community organization (1 = yes).

In logistic regression, the outcome is binary
Odds ratio greater than 1 -> higher odds
Odds ratio less than 1 -> lower odds
Odds ratio equal to 1 -> no association
A quick interpretation shortcut is (OR - 1) x 100

Examples

1.38 -> about 38% higher odds
0.76 -> about 24% lower odds




Sample interpretation — Age


Each additional year of age is associated with odds of participating in a community organization that are about 3% higher, holding income and marital status constant.

title: Week 16
text:
Interactions and Margins Plots
An interaction asks whether the relationship between X and Y depends on a third variable.


Parallel lines suggest little or no moderation
Non-parallel lines suggest the slope changes across groups or levels
The lower-order terms and the interaction term all matter for interpretation




Sample interpretation


Group A’s education slope (1.2) is steeper than Group B’s (0.4) — group membership moderates the relationship between education and the outcome.

title: Week 16
text:
Moderation vs. Mediation









Moderation
Mediation




Big question
For whom or under what conditions does X affect Y?
How or why does X affect Y?


Third variable
Changes the slope
Carries the effect


Typical model
Interaction term
Pathway story


Visual idea
Non-parallel lines
X -> M -> Y


Example
Education predicts income differently for men and women
Education raises income partly through job prestige






Quick test


Draw the arrows. If the third variable is between X and Y, think mediation. If it changes the slope of X -> Y, think moderation.

title: Week 16
text:
Sampling and Figure Critique


Sampling

A representative sample resembles the target population on the characteristics that matter
In cluster sampling you randomly select groups or locations first, then observe people within them (e.g., randomly select hospitals, then survey all patients inside)
Nonresponse or selection bias happens when some kinds of people are systematically harder to reach than others


Figure critique

Does the graph type match the variable?
Are the axes labeled clearly?
Is the title accurate?
Is the scale misleading or truncated in a way that exaggerates differences?
Are the units clear?

title: Week 16
section: How to Study Efficiently This Week
text:
How to Study Efficiently This Week

Revisit the slides and assignments on these topics:

Weeks 7-9: sampling, distributions, CLT, confidence intervals, hypothesis tests
Week 11: OLS regression and R^2
Week 12: logistic regression and odds ratios
Week 13: interactions (moderation) and margins plots

Redo one example each of a t-test, an OLS model, a logistic model, and an interaction model
Practice turning output into short written interpretations
Review definitions you want to be able to write cleanly from memory:

p-value
confidence interval
baseline/reference group
moderation
representative sample





Best use of your time


Do not just reread code. Practice explaining what results mean.

title: Week 16
text:
Questions?

What topics still feel fuzzy?
Which type of output do you want to practice interpreting one more time?

title: Week 13
text:
Visualizing Interaction Terms

Continuous × ContinuousContinuous × BinaryBinary × Binary


Model: lm(income91 ~ prestg80 * age) — does the income payoff of occupational prestige depend on career stage?
People often visualize the dependency of one variable on the other by breaking the variable of interest into different groups. Here, age is broken into three relevant analytical groups; if there are no clear breaks, use standard deviations.


Code
p_int <- ggpredict(model_prestige, terms = c("prestg80", "age [30, 45, 60]")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = predicted, color = group, fill = group)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 0.15, color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.4) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("#E15759", "#4E79A7", "#59A14F"),
    labels = c("Age 30", "Age 45", "Age 60"),
    name   = "Age"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("#E15759", "#4E79A7", "#59A14F"),
    labels = c("Age 30", "Age 45", "Age 60"),
    name   = "Age"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar_format(big.mark = ",")) +
  labs(
    title    = "With interaction",
    subtitle = "Slope varies by age — does prestige compound over a career?",
    x = "Occupational Prestige Score",
    y = "Predicted Income ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p_int












Reading the plot


Each line shows the prestige → income slope at a different career stage (30, 45, 60).

Parallel lines = no interaction, prestige pays off the same regardless of age.
Diverging lines = interaction, the prestige premium grows (or shrinks) with age.

Here we see steeper lines for older ages as the coefficent for the interaction suggests, but the coefficient is not significant. Always check the confidence intervals: the interaction is significant wherever there is no overlap – no real difference in this figure.





Model: lm(income91 ~ educ * sex_f) — does the income return to education differ by sex?
This is simpler to visualize since the categories are already decided for you, unlike with a continuous by continuous interaction.


Code
ggpredict(model_interact, terms = c("educ [4:20 by=0.5]", "sex_f")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = predicted, color = group, fill = group)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), alpha = 0.15, color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.4) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("male" = "#4E79A7", "female" = "#E15759"),
    labels = c("Men", "Women"), name = "Sex"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("male" = "#4E79A7", "female" = "#E15759"),
    labels = c("Men", "Women"), name = "Sex"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar_format(big.mark = ",")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted Income by Education and Sex",
    subtitle = "lm(income91 ~ educ * sex_f) — interaction non-significant",
    x = "Years of Education",
    y = "Predicted Income ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")












Reading the plot


The two lines show the income slope for men (blue) and women (red) across years of education. We know from the model output that the interaction is not significant and the lines are basically parallel. Additionally, the CIs overlap at nearly all points.





Model: lm(income ~ Black * female) — does the racial income gap differ by sex?


Code
ggpredict(model_bb, terms = c("Black", "female")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = predicted, color = group, group = group)) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high), width = 0.08, linewidth = 1.0) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Men" = "#4E79A7", "Women" = "#E15759"), name = "Sex"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar_format(big.mark = ",")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Predicted Income by Race and Sex",
    subtitle = "lm(income ~ Black * female) — interaction: non-additivity of race and sex gaps",
    x = "Race",
    y = "Predicted Income ($)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")












Reading the plot


The drop from White to Black on each line represents the racial income gap. Parallel lines mean the gap is identical for men and women — no interaction. Non-parallel lines suggest the gap differs by sex.
Black × Women (\(\beta_3\) = $2,683, p = 0.106) is not statistically significant — we cannot conclude that the racial income gap actually differs by sex. Any non-parallelism visible in the plot is consistent with sampling noise.

title: Week 16
text:
Moderation







How to Identify
What Moderation does




Big question
For whom or under what conditions does X affect Y?


Third variable
Changes the slope


Typical model
Interaction term


Visual idea
Non-parallel lines


Example
Education predicts income differently for men and women






Quick test


If a third variable changes the slope of X -> Y across groups or levels, that is moderation. Look for non-parallel lines in a margins plot.

title: Week 16
section: Examples of Choosing the Right Tool
text:
Examples of Choosing the Right Tool








Goal
What the variables look like
Good choice




Show the distribution of one categorical variable
One categorical variable
Bar chart


Show the distribution of one quantitative variable
One continuous/count variable
Histogram


Compare the mean of a quantitative outcome across two groups
Quantitative Y + binary group
Two-sample t-test


Study the relationship between two categorical variables
Categorical X + categorical Y
Chi-square test


Model a continuous outcome
Quantitative Y
OLS regression


Model a binary outcome
Y = 0/1
Logistic regression






On the exam


Many mistakes come from choosing the wrong tool because the student did not first identify the outcome type.

title: Week 16
section: Interactions and Margins Plots — Sample Interpretation
text:
Interactions and Margins Plots — Sample Interpretation



Sample interpretation — Interaction


The relationship between education and the expected outcome is stronger for Group A than for Group B. For Group A, each additional unit of education is associated with a 1.2-point increase in the expected outcome. For Group B, that same increase in education is associated with only a 0.4-point increase. The non-parallel lines indicate that group membership moderates the association between education and the outcome.

title: Week 16
section: Interactions and Margins Plots — Key Points
text:
Interactions and Margins Plots — Key Points

Parallel lines suggest little or no moderation
Non-parallel lines suggest the slope changes across groups or levels
The lower-order terms and the interaction term all matter for interpretation
A margins plot helps you describe the pattern visually, even before you write out the coefficients




Sample interpretation — Interaction


The relationship between education and the expected outcome is stronger for Group A than for Group B. For Group A, each additional unit of education is associated with a 1.2-point increase in the expected outcome. For Group B, that same increase in education is associated with only a 0.4-point increase. The non-parallel lines indicate that group membership moderates the association between education and the outcome.
